seo.title: Cybersecurity Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for code generation in cybersecurity environments introduces compounding risks: a single misconfigured prompt or unsandboxed execution can expose threat intelligence, introduce exploitable vulnerabilities, or trigger automated incident response cascades. This checklist operationalizes NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directive requirements specifically for LLM-driven code generation pipelines handling sensitive security data. Following these controls is not optional — in adversarial environments, gaps in AI deployment hygiene become attack surfaces.

sections.heading: Threat Intelligence Data Isolation & Prompt Security
sections.description: Prevent adversary exploitation of AI-leaked intelligence by enforcing strict data boundaries between threat intel sources and LLM API surfaces.

items.label: Classify and tag all threat intelligence inputs before LLM ingestion
items.description: Apply TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) classifications to all threat data (IOCs, TTPs, CVEs) before it enters any LLM prompt. Enforce automated tagging via your SIEM or SOAR pipeline so TLP:RED and TLP:AMBER data is blocked from external LLM API calls entirely.

items.label: Prohibit external LLM API calls for classified or sensitive threat data
items.description: Mandate that any prompt containing internal threat intelligence, active incident data, or classified adversary TTPs must route exclusively to on-premises or private-cloud-hosted LLM deployments. Implement network egress rules that block threat intel data from reaching third-party LLM endpoints.

items.label: Implement prompt injection detection before code generation execution
items.description: Deploy a dedicated prompt injection scanner (e.g., Rebuff, custom regex + semantic classifiers) on all user-supplied inputs to the code generation agent. Log and quarantine any prompt that attempts to extract system instructions, override safety rules, or reference internal network topology.

items.label: Redact credentials, API keys, and internal hostnames from LLM context windows
items.description: Run all code generation prompts through an automated secrets-detection layer (e.g., truffleHog patterns, custom regex for internal IP ranges and hostnames) before submission to the LLM. Fail the request and alert the operator if any credential pattern is detected.

items.label: Enforce context window minimization for threat intel references
items.description: Limit the amount of threat intelligence included in any single LLM context window to the minimum necessary for the code generation task. Use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with access-controlled vector stores rather than embedding raw threat reports directly in prompts.

items.label: Audit all LLM API request and response payloads for data leakage
items.description: Log every LLM API request and response payload to an immutable, access-controlled audit store (aligned with SOC 2 CC7.2 and ISO 27001 A.12.4). Run automated post-hoc scans on response logs to detect if the model inadvertently echoed sensitive input data in its output.

items.label: Establish a data residency policy for LLM-processed security artifacts
items.description: Document and enforce where LLM-processed code artifacts, prompts, and responses are stored, ensuring alignment with CISA data handling directives and any applicable sector-specific regulations (e.g., FedRAMP for federal environments). Prohibit storage of security artifacts in LLM provider training pipelines via contractual opt-outs.

sections.heading: Sandboxed Code Execution & Dependency Safety
sections.description: Ensure all AI-generated code is executed in isolated environments with validated dependencies before any deployment to production or staging security systems.

items.label: Execute all AI-generated code exclusively in ephemeral, network-isolated sandboxes
items.description: Provision ephemeral containers (e.g., gVisor, Firecracker microVMs) with no outbound network access for every code execution cycle. Destroy and reprovision the sandbox after each test run. Never execute AI-generated security tooling directly on host systems or systems with access to production networks.

items.label: Scan all AI-generated code for hardcoded secrets before sandbox execution
items.description: Integrate a secrets-detection tool (e.g., detect-secrets, Semgrep secret rules) as a pre-execution gate. Block execution and flag for human review if any API key, password, private key, or internal credential pattern is detected in the generated code.

items.label: Validate all third-party dependencies against a curated, approved package allowlist
items.description: Before installing any dependency referenced in AI-generated code, check it against an internally maintained allowlist of approved packages with pinned versions. Reject any package not on the allowlist or with known CVEs in the NVD/OSV databases. Use tools like pip-audit, npm audit, or Dependabot for automated CVE cross-referencing.

items.label: Run static application security testing (SAST) on all AI-generated code before execution
items.description: Integrate SAST tools (e.g., Semgrep, Bandit for Python, CodeQL) into the code generation pipeline as a mandatory pre-execution gate. Fail the pipeline and require human review for any high or critical severity finding, including injection vulnerabilities, insecure deserialization, and path traversal patterns.

items.label: Enforce resource limits and syscall filtering in sandbox environments
items.description: Apply strict cgroup resource limits (CPU, memory, disk I/O) and seccomp syscall filtering profiles to all sandbox containers executing AI-generated code. Block dangerous syscalls (e.g., ptrace, mount, socket creation) that could enable sandbox escape or lateral movement.

items.label: Validate syntax and functional correctness via automated test suites before promotion
items.description: Require AI-generated code to pass a defined suite of unit and integration tests with measurable coverage thresholds (minimum 80% line coverage for security-critical functions) before it can be promoted out of the sandbox. Reject and re-prompt the agent if tests fail, logging the failure reason for audit.

items.label: Implement software bill of materials (SBOM) generation for all AI-generated artifacts
items.description: Automatically generate a CycloneDX or SPDX SBOM for every AI-generated code artifact that passes sandbox validation. Store SBOMs in a version-controlled repository and cross-reference against CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog before any deployment approval.

items.label: Log all sandbox execution events to a tamper-evident SIEM pipeline
items.description: Stream all sandbox execution logs (process creation, file writes, network attempts, exit codes) to your SIEM in real time. Correlate sandbox anomalies (e.g., unexpected network connection attempts by generated code) with active threat intelligence to detect potential supply chain compromise in AI-generated artifacts.

sections.heading: Human Approval Gates & Automated Response Controls
sections.description: Enforce mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints for all AI-generated code and automated actions targeting critical security systems, per CISA directives and NIST CSF RS.CO controls.

items.label: Require named human approver sign-off before deploying AI-generated code to any production security system
items.description: Implement a four-eyes approval workflow in your CI/CD pipeline requiring a named, authenticated security engineer to explicitly approve AI-generated code before it reaches any production system. Approvals must be logged with timestamp, approver identity, and artifact hash in an immutable audit trail (SOC 2 CC6.1).

items.label: Block automated execution of AI-generated incident response actions on critical systems without human approval
items.description: Define a 'critical system' inventory (e.g., firewalls, EDR platforms, identity providers, SCADA/ICS interfaces) and enforce a hard block on any automated action targeting these systems until a human operator explicitly approves the action in your SOAR platform. Log all blocked actions and approvals.

items.label: Implement tiered automation levels based on action risk classification
items.description: Classify all potential automated actions into risk tiers (e.g., Tier 1: read-only queries — auto-approved; Tier 2: non-critical system changes — supervisor approval; Tier 3: critical system changes or response actions — CISO-level approval required). Encode these tiers in your SOAR playbook logic and enforce them programmatically.

items.label: Set mandatory timeout and auto-escalation for pending human approval requests
items.description: Configure all human approval requests to auto-escalate to a secondary approver after 15 minutes and to the on-call security lead after 30 minutes. If no approval is received within 60 minutes for a critical action, automatically revert to a safe default state and trigger a P1 incident ticket.

items.label: Require dual-control approval for AI-generated code that modifies authentication or authorization logic
items.description: Any AI-generated code touching IAM policies, authentication flows, RBAC configurations, or cryptographic key management must require approval from both a security engineer and a compliance officer before sandbox promotion or deployment. Log both approvals with artifact hash.

items.label: Maintain a rollback-ready deployment state for all AI-generated security tool deployments
items.description: Before deploying any AI-generated security tool or script, capture a verified snapshot or configuration backup of the target system. Ensure the rollback procedure is tested and documented, with a maximum rollback time objective (RTO) of 15 minutes for critical security systems.

items.label: Conduct post-deployment review of all AI-generated code within 24 hours of production deployment
items.description: Schedule a mandatory post-deployment review meeting within 24 hours of any AI-generated code reaching production. Review runtime behavior logs, SIEM alerts, and any anomalies. Document findings and update the AI agent's system prompt constraints or guardrails if unexpected behaviors are observed.

sections.heading: AI-Generated Advisory Verification & CVE Compliance
sections.description: Ensure all AI-generated security advisories, vulnerability assessments, and remediation recommendations are verified against authoritative CVE and threat databases before distribution or action.

items.label: Cross-reference all AI-generated vulnerability references against NVD and MITRE CVE databases before publishing
items.description: Implement an automated post-generation validation step that extracts all CVE identifiers from AI-generated advisories and queries the NVD API and MITRE CVE database to verify existence, severity score (CVSS), and current status. Reject or flag any advisory referencing non-existent or hallucinated CVE IDs.

items.label: Validate AI-generated CVSS scores against official NVD CVSS vectors
items.description: For every CVE referenced in AI-generated code comments, advisories, or remediation scripts, programmatically fetch the official CVSS v3.1 base score and vector string from NVD. If the AI-generated score deviates by more than 1.0 from the official score, flag for human review before the advisory is acted upon.

items.label: Check all referenced CVEs against the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog
items.description: Integrate a daily-refreshed local copy of the CISA KEV catalog into the advisory validation pipeline. Automatically escalate the priority of any AI-generated advisory or remediation script that references a KEV-listed vulnerability, triggering immediate human review and expedited patching workflows.

items.label: Prohibit distribution of AI-generated security advisories without human expert review and sign-off
items.description: Enforce a policy that no AI-generated security advisory, threat report, or vulnerability notification may be distributed internally or externally without review and digital signature from a named, credentialed security analyst. Implement DLP controls to block unsigned AI-generated advisories from reaching email or ticketing systems.

items.label: Validate AI-generated remediation code against vendor-published security patches and advisories
items.description: Before executing any AI-generated remediation script, cross-reference the proposed fix against the official vendor security advisory (e.g., Microsoft MSRC, Red Hat Security Advisories, vendor CVE pages). Flag and block execution if the AI-generated fix contradicts or deviates significantly from the vendor-recommended remediation.

items.label: Maintain a versioned audit log of all AI-generated advisories and their verification outcomes
items.description: Store every AI-generated advisory, its CVE verification results, human review outcome, and final disposition in a versioned, immutable audit log. Retain logs for a minimum of 3 years to support SOC 2 Type II audit evidence requirements and ISO 27001 A.12.4 compliance.

items.label: Implement false positive tracking and feedback loop for AI-generated threat assessments
items.description: Track all AI-generated threat assessments that result in false positive incident responses. Log the false positive rate per agent version and model configuration. If the false positive rate exceeds 5% over a 30-day rolling window, trigger a mandatory model revalidation and prompt engineering review before continued deployment.

notes.framework: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF 2.0) — Govern & Protect Functions
notes.note: Under CSF 2.0's new Govern function (GV.OC, GV.RM), organizations must establish AI-specific risk management policies for automated security tooling. For code generation agents, this requires documented risk acceptance criteria for AI-generated artifacts, defined roles for human oversight, and integration of AI risk into the broader organizational cybersecurity risk register. The Protect function (PR.DS) requires that threat intelligence data processed by LLMs be classified and protected with controls equivalent to those applied to the original data source.

notes.framework: SOC 2 Type II — CC6 (Logical and Physical Access) & CC7 (System Operations)
notes.note: SOC 2 CC6.1 requires that access to AI systems processing security data be restricted to authorized personnel with documented access reviews. CC6.6 mandates that all inputs to security-relevant systems (including LLM prompts containing threat data) be validated and logged. CC7.2 requires that security events generated by AI agents — including sandbox execution anomalies and failed code validation gates — be detected, logged, and responded to within defined SLAs. Auditors will expect evidence of human approval workflows for critical automated actions.

notes.framework: ISO 27001:2022 — A.8.25 (Secure Development Lifecycle) & A.5.23 (Information Security for Cloud Services)
notes.note: ISO 27001:2022 Annex A.8.25 requires that secure coding principles be applied throughout the development lifecycle, including AI-assisted code generation. Organizations must document how AI-generated code is reviewed, tested, and validated against security requirements. A.5.23 mandates that cloud-based LLM services used for code generation be assessed for information security risks, with contractual controls ensuring threat intelligence data is not used for model training and data residency requirements are met.

notes.framework: CISA Binding Operational Directives (BOD) & Emergency Directives
notes.note: CISA BOD 22-01 (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) creates a mandatory obligation for federal agencies and a strong best-practice expectation for critical infrastructure operators to remediate KEV-listed vulnerabilities within defined timeframes. AI-generated remediation code must be validated against KEV catalog entries to ensure compliance with these timelines. CISA's AI cybersecurity guidance (2024) explicitly warns against deploying AI systems that can autonomously execute actions on critical infrastructure without human oversight, directly supporting the human approval gate requirements in this checklist.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Deploy Vex to continuously enforce these controls across your AI code generation pipeline — automating CVE verification, sandbox policy enforcement, human approval workflows, and audit logging so your security team can ship faster without compromising compliance posture.
